title: SEGMENT 9 — Monoclonal antibodies — the hybridoma breakthrough

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Monoclonal antibodies — the hybridoma breakthrough

In 1975, in a small lab at the Medical Research Council in Cambridge, England, two scientists named Georges Köhler and César Milstein did an experiment that, in retrospect, looks almost embarrassingly simple.
It changed medicine forever, and it won them the Nobel Prize in 1984.
Their problem was this.
They wanted antibodies of a single, defined specificity, in unlimited supply.
They could get B cells that made the antibody they wanted — just immunize a mouse against the target, and somewhere in its spleen there would be B cells producing the right antibody.
The problem was that B cells, once removed from a mouse, don't survive long in culture.
They die within days.
So you can isolate the right B cell, but you can't keep it alive long enough to harvest meaningful quantities of antibody.
Meanwhile, scientists had separately developed cancerous B cell lines called myelomas — these are essentially B cells gone malignant, and the relevant feature is that they're immortal.
They grow forever in a culture dish.
The catch: myeloma cells don't produce a useful antibody.
They produce nonsense, or random myeloma protein, but not the antibody you actually want.
So Köhler and Milstein had two cells, each with half of what they wanted.
The normal B cell could make the right antibody but couldn't survive.
The myeloma could survive forever but couldn't make the right antibody.
The question was: could you somehow combine them?
Here is the analogy, and I love this analogy.
Think of it as a one-time mating between a mortal antibody-maker and an immortal cell — and the child inherits both gifts.
The B cell brings the right antibody-making machinery.
The myeloma brings the ability to live and divide forever.
The hybrid child does both at once.
How do you mate two cells?
You can't ask politely.
The technique Köhler and Milstein used was to add a chemical, polyethylene glycol — PEG — that physically encourages cells to fuse their membranes together.
When you mix B cells with myeloma cells and add PEG, a small fraction of the cells fuse, producing hybrid cells with combined cytoplasm and both sets of chromosomes.
These hybrids are called hybridomas — hybrid plus myeloma.

Now you need to select for the hybrids and against the un-fused parents — most of the cells in the dish are still pure B cells (which will die anyway) or pure myeloma cells (which would grow and overrun your culture).

Köhler and Milstein used a clever metabolic trick involving a drug called HAT that kills the parent myeloma but leaves hybridomas alive — the molecular details aren't important — the point is, after the selection, only hybridomas survive.
They're immortal, like the myeloma parent, and they secrete antibody, inherited from the B cell parent.
But each hybridoma is the fusion of one specific B cell with one myeloma, and the spleen of the immunized mouse contained millions of B cells with millions of different antibodies.
So your dish of surviving hybridomas is a mixture — each individual hybridoma makes a different antibody, but all of them are immortal and all of them make antibody.
You then clone them — dilute the cells so that single hybridomas end up in separate wells of a culture plate — and let each single cell grow into a colony.
Now each colony is descended from one ancestor cell, and produces exactly one antibody.
You screen each colony to find the one making the antibody you want.

Pick that colony, expand it into a culture flask, then a bioreactor — and you now have an immortal cell line that pumps out, day after day, year after year, identical molecules of your chosen antibody.
That's a monoclonal antibody.
Mono — meaning one — clone — meaning all derived from a single ancestor cell.
Every molecule produced is structurally identical to every other.
You've turned the locksmith into the factory.
The implications were enormous, and they unfolded over the following decades.

In research, monoclonal antibodies became the standard tool for labeling and tracking specific proteins — they're the reason your textbook diagrams of cells have proteins in different colors, because monoclonals can be tagged with fluorescent dyes and used to light up exactly one protein at a time.
In diagnostics, monoclonals became the basis of pregnancy tests, COVID rapid tests, blood-type matching, cancer biomarker assays — essentially every immunoassay you can think of.
And in therapy — well, we're going to spend the rest of this lecture on the therapy side, because that's where the revolution is loudest.
The first therapeutic monoclonal antibody, OKT3, was approved in 1986.
It was used to suppress organ transplant rejection.
There are now well over a hundred therapeutic monoclonals approved, hitting essentially every major disease area in medicine.
But — and there's always a but — the original Köhler-Milstein hybridoma technology had a major limitation that the field had to wrestle with for the next thirty years.
The B cells they fused came from a mouse.

The antibody was therefore a mouse antibody, and when you injected a mouse antibody into a human, the same problem that plagued horse serum a century earlier reared up: the human immune system saw a foreign protein and attacked it.
Patients developed what was called HAMA — human anti-mouse antibody — reactions that limited efficacy, caused side effects, and prevented repeat dosing.
The first mouse monoclonals worked, but they couldn't be used long-term.
To fix this, the field had to figure out how to make the antibody less mouse and more human, eventually fully human, while still keeping the specificity that came from the mouse B cell.

That fix took two parallel tracks of innovation — protein engineering through humanization, and totally new methods of generating human antibodies from scratch in test tubes — and that's where we'll go after the break.
